title: Chapter 1
html:
Forward

Edgar Rice Burroughs' Pellucidar (1915) continues the subterranean saga initiated in At the Earth's Core, delving deeper into the hollow world of Pellucidar. This critique examines the novel's thematic explorations, character dynamics, and its place within the broader context of early 20th-century adventure literature.

The Hollow Earth as a Metaphor

Pellucidar's unique geography—a world within our own, illuminated by a central sun and devoid of a conventional sense of time—serves as a rich metaphor for the subconscious and the uncharted territories of the human psyche. The perpetual daylight and absence of temporal markers challenge the protagonists' perceptions, symbolizing a journey into the timeless realm of the inner self.

"Time is a meaningless term in Pellucidar, where the sun hangs perpetually at zenith."
— Chapter Three

This setting invites readers to consider the constructs of time and civilization, and how their absence affects human behavior and societal development.

David Innes: The Reluctant Conqueror

David Innes emerges as a complex figure, embodying both the virtues and vices of imperial ambition. His quest to unite the disparate tribes of Pellucidar under a centralized government mirrors contemporary colonial endeavors, raising questions about the ethics of imposing order upon 'savage' lands.

"I had dreamed of a united Pellucidar, of a federation of the free peoples of the inner world."
— Chapter Ten

Innes' vision, while noble in intent, reflects the paternalistic attitudes prevalent during Burroughs' time, prompting modern readers to scrutinize the implications of such narratives.

Dian the Beautiful: Agency and Archetype

Dian the Beautiful transcends the typical damsel-in-distress trope, displaying resilience and autonomy throughout the narrative. Her character challenges the gender norms of early 20th-century literature, offering a more nuanced portrayal of femininity within the adventure genre.

"Dian was no weakling to be protected; she was a warrior in her own right."
— Chapter Twelve

Dian's strength and independence contribute to the novel's progressive elements, highlighting Burroughs' occasional subversion of genre conventions.

The Mahars: Intelligence Without Emotion

The Mahars, an advanced reptilian species ruling over Pellucidar, represent a civilization that has prioritized intellect at the expense of emotion and empathy. Their cold, calculated dominance serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of valuing knowledge over humanity.

"The Mahars had long since ceased to feel; their intellects ruled supreme, devoid of compassion."
— Chapter Fifteen

This depiction invites reflection on the balance between reason and emotion in the governance of societies.

Imperialism and Its Discontents

Burroughs' narrative grapples with the complexities of imperialism, simultaneously endorsing and critiquing the expansionist ideologies of his era. While Innes' efforts to civilize Pellucidar are portrayed as heroic, the resistance he encounters underscores the inherent tensions and ethical dilemmas of such endeavors.

"Not all welcomed our vision of unity; many clung to their ancient ways, wary of our intentions."
— Chapter Eighteen

This ambivalence adds depth to the narrative, encouraging readers to question the morality of cultural imposition.

Conclusion

Pellucidar offers a multifaceted exploration of adventure, identity, and societal structures. Through its imaginative setting and complex characters, the novel transcends its pulp origins, inviting readers to ponder profound questions about civilization, power, and the human condition.
